1.1: FROM of old God spoke to our fathers by the prophets in every manner and in all ways; and in these latter days, he has spoken to us by his Son;
1.2: Whom he has appointed heir of all things, and by whom also he made the worlds;
1.3: For he is the brightness of his glory and the express image of his being, upholding all things by the power of his word; and when he had through his person, cleansed our sins, then he sat down on the right hand of the Majesty on high;
1.4: And he is altogether greater than the angels, just as the name he has inherited is a more excellent name than theirs.
1.5: For to which of the angels has God at any time, said You are my Son, this day have I begotten you? And again, I will be to him a Father, and he shall be to me a Son?
1.6: And again, when he brought the Firstbegotten into the world, he said, Let all the angels of God worship him.
1.7: And of the angels he said thus, Who makes his angels spirits; his ministers a flaming fire.
1.8: But of the Son he said, Thy throne, O God, is for ever and ever: the scepter of thy kingdom is a right scepter.
1.9: You have loved righteousness, and hated iniquity; therefore, God, even your God, has anointed you with the oil of gladness more than your fellows.
1.10: And from the very beginning you have laid the foundations of the earth; and the heavens are the works of your hands:
1.11: They shall pass away; but you shall endure; and they all shall wear out like a garment;
1.12: And as a cloak you shall fold them up, and they shall be changed: but you are the same, and your years shall never end.
1.13: For to which of the angels has he at any time said, Sit thou at my right hand, until I make thine enemies thy footstool under your feet?
1.14: Are they not all ministering spirits, sent forth in the service for those who shall inherit life everlasting?

2.1: THEREFORE, we should give earnest heed to the things which we have heard, lest at any time they be lost.
2.2: For if the word spoken by the angels has been affirmed, and every one who has heard it and transgressed it has received a just reward,
2.3: How shall we escape, if we neglect the very things which are our salvation and which were first spoken by our LORD, and were proved to us by those who had heard him,
2.4: And to which God testified with signs and wonders and with divers miracles, and with the gift of the Holy Spirit, given according to his will.
2.5: For he has not put into subjection to the angels the world to come, whereof we speak.
2.6: But as the scripture testifies, saying, What is man that thou art mindful of him? and the son of man, that thou visitest him?
2.7: For thou hast made him a little lower than the angels: and have crowned him with glory and honor, and have set him ruler over the works of thy hands:
2.8: Thou hast put all things in subjection under his feet. By putting all things under his control, he left nothing that he did not put under subjection to him. But now we do not see yet that all things are in subjection to him.
2.9: We see that he is Jesus who humbled himself to be a little lower than the angels, through his suffering and his death, but now he is crowned with glory and honor; for he tasted death for the sake of every one but God.
2.10: And it was meet and proper for him, in whose hand is everything and for whom are all things, to bring many sons to glory, so that from the very beginning of their salvation, they are made perfect through sufferings.
2.11: For both he who sanctifies and those who are sanctified are all of one origin: for this reason he is not ashamed to call them brethren,
2.12: Saying, I will declare thy name to my brethren; in the midst of the congregation will I praise thee.
2.13: And again, I will put my trust in him. And again, Behold me and the children which God has given me.
2.14: Forasmuch then as the children are partakers of flesh and blood, he also likewise partook of the same; and by his death he has destroyed him who had the power of death, that is, the devil.
2.15: And has released them who, through fear of death all their lives, were subject to slavery.
2.16: For he did not take on him the pattern of angels; but he did take on him the seed of Abraham.
2.17: Therefore it was meet and proper that in every thing he should resemble his brethren, that he might be a merciful, and faithful high priest in the things of God, to make reconciliation for the sins of the people.
2.18: For in that he himself has suffered, being tempted, he is able to help those who are tempted.

3.1: FROM henceforth, O my holy brethren, called by a call from heaven, look to this Apostle and High Priest of our faith, Jesus Christ:
3.2: Who was faithful to him who appointed him, as also Moses was faithful to all his house.
3.3: The glory of Jesus is much greater than that of Moses, just as the honor of the builder of the house is greater than the house.
3.4: For every house is built by some man; but he who builds all things is God.
3.5: And Moses, as a servant, was faithful to all his house, and was a testimony of those things which were to be spoken after;
3.6: But Christ, as a son, over his own house, whose house we are, if to the end we hold fast with confidence to the glory of his hope.
3.7: Therefore, as the Holy Spirit said, Today if you will hear his voice,
3.8: Harden not your hearts to provoke him, as the murmurers did in the day of temptation in the wilderness:
3.9: Your fathers tempted me even though they examined and saw my works forty years.
3.10: Therefore I was not pleased with that generation, and said, These are a people whose hearts have been misled and they have not known my ways.
3.11: So I swore in my anger, They shall not enter into my rest.
3.12: Take heed therefore, my brethren, lest perhaps there is a man among you who has an evil heart and is not a believer, and you will be cut off from the living God.
3.13: But search your hearts daily, until the day which is called, The day; to the end that no man among you be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin.
3.14: For we are made partakers of Christ, if from the beginning to the very end we hold steadfast to this true covenant,
3.15: As it is said, Today, if you hear even the echoes of his voice, do not harden your hearts to anger him.
3.16: Who are those who have heard and provoked him? Were they not those who came out of Egypt under Moses, although not all of them?
3.17: But with whom was he displeased for forty years? Was it not especially with those who had sinned and whose bones lay in the wilderness?
3.18: And against whom did he swear that they should not enter into his rest, except against those who did not listen?
3.19: So we see that they could not enter in because they did not believe.

4.1: LET us therefore fear, while the prom ise of entering into his rest remains, lest some amongst you find they are prevented from entering.
4.2: For the gospel was preached to us, as it was to them also, but the word they heard did not benefit them: because it was not mixed with faith in those who heard it.
4.3: But we who have believed will enter into rest, as he said, As I have sworn in my wrath, they shall not enter into my rest; for behold, the works of God were from the very foundation of the world.
4.4: For he said concerning the sabbath, God rested on the seventh day from all his works.
4.5: And here again he said, They shall not enter into my rest.
4.6: There was a chance for some to enter therein, but they to whom the gospel was first preached did not enter, because they would not listen:
4.7: And again, after a long time he appointed another day, as it is written above, for David said, Today if you hear his voice, harden not your hearts.
4.8: For if Joshua the son of Nun had given them rest, he would not afterward have spoken of another day.
4.9: It is therefore the duty of the people of God to keep the Sabbath.
4.10: For he who has entered into his rest, he also has ceased from his own works, as God did from his.
4.11: Let us strive therefore to enter into that rest, lest any man fall like those who were disobedient.
4.12: For the word of God is living and powerful and sharper than any twoedged sword, piercing even to the point of division between soul and spirit, and between the joints and marrow and bones, and is a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart.
4.13: And there is no creature which is hidden from his sight: but all things are naked and open before the eyes of him to whom we are to answer.
4.14: We have, therefore, a great high priest who has ascended into heaven, Jesus Christ, the Son of God; let us remain firm in his faith.
4.15: For we do not have a high priest who cannot share our infirmities, but we have one who was tempted with everything as we are, and yet without sin.
4.16: Let us, therefore, come openly to the throne of his grace, that we may obtain mercy, and find grace to help in time of need.

5.1: FOR every high priest chosen from among men is ordained on behalf of men about things pertaining to God, that he may offer both gifts and sacrifices for sins:
5.2: He is one who can humble himself and have compassion on those who are ignorant and go astray: for he himself also is subject to weaknesses.
5.3: Because of these, he is obliged, just as he offers sacrifices for the people, likewise to offer for himself on account of his own sins.
5.4: And no man takes this honor unto himself, but only he who is called of God as was Aar’on.
5.5: So also Christ did not glorify himself by becoming a high priest, but he glorified him who said to him, Thou art my Son, Today have I begotten thee.
5.6: And he said also in another place, Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchis'e- dec.
5.7: Even when he was clothed in the flesh, he offered prayers and supplications, with vehement cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death; and verily he was heard.
5.8: And though he were a good Son, because of fear and suffering which he endured, he learned obedience.
5.9: And he grew to be perfect, and became the author of life everlasting to all who obey him;
5.10: So he was called by God, a high priest after the order of Mel-chis'e-dec.
5.11: Now concerning this very Mel-chis'edec, we have much to say, but it is difficult to explain because you are dull of comprehension.
5.12: By now you should be teachers because you have been a long time in training: But even now you need to be taught the primary writings of the word of God; but you are still in need of milk, and not strong meat.
5.13: For every man whose food is milk, is unfamiliar with the word of righteousness: for he is a babe.
5.14: But strong meat belongs to those who are of full age, even those who by reason of use have their senses exercised to discern both good and evil.

6.1: THEREFORE, let us leave the elementary word of Christ, and let us go on to perfection: Why do you again lay another foundation for the repentance from past deeds, and for faith in God?
6.2: And for the doctrine of baptisms, and for the laying on of hands, and for the resurrection of the dead and for eternal judgment?
6.3: If the LORD permits, this we will do.
6.4: But this is impossible for those who have once been baptised, and have tasted the gift from heaven, and have received the Holy Spirit,
6.5: And have tasted the good word of God, and the powers of the world to come,
6.6: For, for them to sin again, and be renewed again by repentance, they crucify the Son of God a second time, and put him to open shame.
6.7: For the earth which drinks in the rain that falls abundantly on it, and brings forth herbs useful to those for whom it is cultivated, receives blessing from God:
6.8: But if it should produce thorns and briers it is rejected and not far from being condemned; and at the end this crop will be used for fuel.
6.9: But beloved brethren, we expect from you the things that are good and that pertain to salvation, even though we speak in this manner.
6.10: For God is not unjust to forget your works and your labor of love which you have made known in his name, for you have ministered to the saints and still do minister.
6.11: We desire that every one of you show the same diligence toward the fulfillment of your hope, even unto the end,
6.12: And that you be not slothful, but be followers of those who through faith and patience have become heirs of the promise.
6.13: For when God made a promise to A’braham, because there was none greater than himself by whom he could swear, he swore by himself,
6.14: Saying, Blessing, I will bless you, and multiplying, I will multiply you.
6.15: And so he was patient, and obtained the promise.
6.16: For men swear by one who is greater than themselves: and in every dispute among them, the true settlement is by oaths.
6.17: Therefore, because God wanted more abundantly to show to the heirs of promise that his agreement was unchangeable, he sealed it by an oath.
6.18: Thus, by the promise and by the oath which are unchangeable, and in neither of which could God lie, we find courage to hold fast to the hope that has been promised by him in whom we have taken refuge.
6.19: That promise is like an anchor to us; it upholds the soul so that it may not be shaken, and it penetrates beyond the veil of the temple;
6.20: Therein Jesus has previously entered for our sakes, and become the high priest for ever, after the order of Mel-chis'e-dec.

7.1: FOR this Mel-chis'e-dec was king of Sa lem, the priest of the most high God, who met A’bra-ham, returning from the slaughter of the kings, and blessed him;
7.2: And to whom A’bra-ham also set aside a tenth part from the choice things he had with him. His name is interpreted King of righteousness, and again, King of Salem, which means King of peace.
7.3: Neither his father nor his mother is recorded in the genealogies; and neither the beginning of his days nor the end of his life; but, like the Son of God, his priesthood abides for ever.
7.4: Now consider how great this man was, unto whom even the patriarch Abraham gave tithes and paid head tax.
7.5: For those sons of Levi who received the office of the priesthood, were authorized by law to take tithes from the people; even from their own brethren who also had come out of the loins of Abraham.
7.6: But this man who is not recorded in their genealogies took tithes even from Abraham, and blessed him who had received the promises.
7.7: Beyond dispute: he who was less was blessed by him who was greater than himself.
7.8: And here mortal men receive tithes; but there he, of whom the scripture testifies that he lives, receives them.
7.9: Speaking as a man, through A’bra-ham, even Levi who received tithes, also gave tithes.
7.10: For he was yet in the loins of his forefather Abraham, when Mel-chis'e-dec met him.
7.11: If therefore perfection had been reached by the Le-vit’ic-al priesthood, by which the law was enacted for the people, what further need was there that another priest should rise after the order of Mel-chis'e-dec? Otherwise, the scriptures would have said, that he would be after the order of Aaron.
7.12: Since there was a change in the priesthood, so also there was a change in the law.
7.13: For he, concerning whom these things are spoken, was born of another tribe, from which no man ever ministered at the altar.
7.14: For it is evident that our LORD sprang out of Juda, of which tribe Moses said nothing concerning the priesthood.
7.15: And yet, it is far more evident because he said that another priest would rise after the order of Mel-chis'e-dec,
7.16: One who was not appointed after the law of carnal commandments, but after the power of life which abides for ever.
7.17: For he testified concerning him, Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchis'e- dec.
7.18: For the change which took place in the former law, was made on account of its weaknesses, and because it had become useless.
7.19: For the law made nothing perfect, but there has come in its place a better hope, by which we draw near to God.
7.20: And he confirmed it for us by oath.
7.21: For they were made priests without oaths; but this one was made a priest with an oath, as it was said concerning him by David, The LORD has sworn, and will not lie, Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Mel-chis'edec.
7.22: All these things make a better covenant because Jesus is its surety.
7.23: And these priests were many, because they were mortal, and they were not permitted to continue because of death:
7.24: But this man, because he is immortal, has a priesthood which remains for ever.
7.25: Therefore he is able to forever save those who come to God by him because forever he lives to make intercession for them.
7.26: For this is the kind of high priest proper for us; pure, without evil, and undefiled, far away from sin, and made higher than the heavens;
7.27: And who needs not daily, as do those high priests, to offer up sacrifice, first for their own sins, and then for the people’s, for this he did once when he offered up himself.
7.28: For the law appoints imperfect men priests; but the word of the oath which came after the law appoints the Son who is perfect for evermore.

8.1: NOW above all we have a high priest, who is seated at the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in heaven;
8.2: And he has become the minister of the sanctuary, and of the true tabernacle, which God pitched and not man.
8.3: For every high priest is appointed to offer gifts and sacrifices, therefore it is necessary that this man have something to offer also.
8.4: For if he were on earth, he would not be a priest, because there are priests who offer gifts according to the law:
8.5: Who serve the semblance and shadow of heavenly things, just as it was commanded to Moses when he was about to make the tabernacle: See that you make all things according to the pattern showed to thee in the mount.
8.6: But now Jesus Christ has received a ministry which is greater than that; just as the covenant in which he was made a mediator, is greater, so are the promises greater than those given in the old covenant.
8.7: For if the first covenant had been faultless, then there would have been no need for the second.
8.8: For he found fault with them, and said, Behold, the day is coming, saith the LORD, when I will perfect a new covenant with the house of Israel and with the house of Judah:
8.9: Not according to the covenant that I made with their fathers in the day when I took them by the hand and led them out of the land of Egypt; and because they abode not in my covenant, I rejected them, saith the LORD.
8.10: For this shall be the covenant that I will make with the house of Israel; After those days, saith the LORD, I will put my law into their minds, and I will write it on their hearts: and I will be their God, and they shall be my people.
8.11: And no man shall teach his neighbor, neither his brother, saying, Know the LORD: for all shall know me, from the youngest to the oldest.
8.12: And I will forgive their wickedness, and I will no longer remember their sins.
8.13: By that which he has said, With a new covenant, the first one has become old, and that which is old and obsolete is near destruction.

9.1: THEN verily the first covenant had also ordinances of divine service and a worldly sanctuary.
9.2: For the first tabernacle which was made, had in it the candlestick, and the table and the shewbread; and it was called the sanctuary.
9.3: But the inner tabernacle, which is within the veil of the second door, was called the Holy of Holies.
9.4: And there was in it the golden censer, and the ark of the covenant all overlaid with gold, and in it was the golden pot containing the manna, and Aaron’s rod which sprouted, and the tablets of the covenant;
9.5: And over it the cherubim of glory, overshadowing the mercy seat; now is not the time to describe how these things were made.
9.6: The priests always entered into the outer tabernacle and performed their service of worship;
9.7: But into the inner tabernacle, the high priest entered alone, once every year, with the blood which he offered for himself, and for the faults of the people.
9.8: By this the Holy Spirit revealed that the way of the saints would not yet be made known, so long as the old tabernacle remained;
9.9: Which was the symbol for that time, now past, in which were offered both gifts and sacrifices, which could not make perfect the conscience of him who offered them,
9.10: But which served only for food and drink, and in divers ablutions, which are ordinances of the flesh and which were imposed until the time of reformation.
9.11: But Christ, who had come, became the high priest of the good things which he wrought; and he entered into a greater and more perfect tabernacle which was not made by hands, and was not of this world;
9.12: And he did not enter with the blood of goats and calves, but by his own blood, he entered in once into the holy place, and obtained for us everlasting redemption.
9.13: For if the blood of goats and calves, and the ashes of a heifer, sprinkled on those who were defiled, sanctified them even to the cleansing of their flesh;
9.14: How much more will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God, purify our conscience from dead works so that we may serve the living God?
9.15: For this cause he became the mediator of the new testament and by his death he became redemption for those who transgressed the old covenant, that those who are called may receive the promise of eternal inheritance.
9.16: For where a testament is, it is proved after the death of its maker.
9.17: For a testament is of force after men are dead, otherwise it is useless so long as its maker lives.
9.18: For this reason not even the first covenant was confirmed without blood.
9.19: For when Moses had given every precept to all the people according to the law, Moses took the blood of a heifer with water, and scarlet wool and hyssop, and sprinkled it on the books and on all the people,
9.20: Saying, This is the blood of the testament, which has been ordained for you by God.
9.21: That very blood he also sprinkled on the tabernacle and on all the vessels used for worship;
9.22: Because nearly everything, according to the law, is purified with the blood: and without shedding of blood there is no forgiveness.
9.23: It is necessary, therefore, that the patterns of things which are heavenly should be purified with these; but the heavenly things themselves, with sacrifices better than these.
9.24: For Christ has not entered into the holy place made with hands, which is the symbol of the true one; but he entered into heaven itself to appear before the presence of God for our sakes.
9.25: Not so that he should offer himself many times, as did the high priest who enters into the holy place every year with blood which is not his own;
9.26: And if not so, then he would have been obliged to suffer many times from the very beginning of the world: but now at the end of the world, only once, by his sacrifice, did he offer himself to abolish sin.
9.27: And just as it is appointed for men to die once, and after their death, the judgment;
9.28: So Christ was once offered to bear the sins of many; so that at his second coming, he shall appear without our sins for the salvation of those who look for him.

10.1: FOR the law had in it a shadow of the good things to come, but was not the essence of the things themselves; hence although the same sacrifices were offered every year, they could not perfect those who offered them.
10.2: For if they had once been perfected, they would have ceased from their offerings; for, from henceforth their minds would not have driven them into the sins from which they had once been cleansed.
10.3: But in those sacrifices they remembered their sins every year.
10.4: For it is not possible that the blood of bulls and of goats could take away sins.
10.5: Therefore, when he entered into the world, he said: Sacrifices and offerings thou didst not desire, but a body thou hast prepared me:
10.6: Burnt offering and sin offering thou has not required.
10.7: Then said I, Lo, I come, in the beginning of the books, it is written of me, I delight to do thy will, O God.
10.8: Above when he said: Sacrifices and offerings and burnt offerings and offerings for sins, thou wouldst not, the very ones which were offered according to the law:
10.9: And after that he said, Lo, I come to do thy will, O God. Thus he put an end to the first in order to establish the second.
10.10: By this very will, we are sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all.
10.11: For every high priest appointed ministered daily, offering the same sacrifices, which had never been able to cleanse sins:
10.12: But this man after he had offered one sacrifice for sins, sat down on the right hand of God for ever.
10.13: From henceforth there he will remain until his enemies are placed as a foot-stool under his feet.
10.14: For by one offering he has perfected for ever those who are sanctified.
10.15: The Holy Spirit is also a witness to us: for he had said before,
10.16: This is the covenant that I will make with them after those days, saith the LORD, I will put my law in their minds, and write it on their hearts.
10.17: And their iniquities and sins will I remember no more.
10.18: For where there is forgiveness of sins, there is no need for offering for sins.
10.19: Having therefore, my brethren, boldness to enter into the holiest by the blood of Jesus,
10.20: By a new and living way, which he has made new for us, through the veil, that is to say, his flesh;
10.21: And having a great high priest over the house of God:
10.22: Let us draw near with a true heart in full assurance of faith, having our hearts sprinkled and cleansed of evil thought, and our bodies washed with pure water.
10.23: Let us remain firm in the profession of our faith without wavering: for he who has promised us is faithful.
10.24: And let us consider one another to arouse love and good works:
10.25: Not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together, as is customary for some; but exhorting one another: and so much the more when you see that day approaching.
10.26: For if any man sin willfully after he has received the knowledge of the truth, then there is no more sacrifice to be offered for sins,
10.27: But he is ready for the fearful judgment and the fiery indignation which shall consume the adversaries.
10.28: He who transgressed the law of Moses, on the word of two or three witnesses; died without mercy:
10.29: How much more punishment do you think he will receive who has trodden underfoot the Son of God, and has considered the blood of his covenant, through which he had been sanctified, as ordinary blood and has blasphemed the Spirit of Grace?
10.30: For we know him who said, Vengeance is mine, and I will repay, saith the LORD. And again, The LORD shall judge his people.
10.31: It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God.
10.32: Remember, therefore, the former days, in which, after you received baptism, you endured a great fight of suffering.
10.33: By reproach and trouble, you were made an object of ridicule; and you have also become companions of those men who have also endured these things.
10.34: And you had pity on those who were prisoners, and you took the seizure of your property cheerfully, for you know in yourselves that you have a better and a more enduring possession in heaven.
10.35: Do not lose, therefore, the confidence that you have, for it has a great reward.
10.36: For you have need of patience in order that you may do the will of God and receive the promise.
10.37: For the time is all too short, and he who is to come will come, and will not delay.
10.38: But the righteous shall live by my faith: and if any draw back, my soul shall have no pleasure in him.
10.39: But we do not belong to those who draw back to perdition, but to the faith which restores our soul.

11.1: NOW faith is the substance of things hoped for, just as it was the substance of things which have come to pass; and it is the evidence of things not seen,
11.2: And in this way it became a testimonial of the elders.
11.3: For it is through faith we understand that the worlds were framed by the word of God, so that the things which are seen came to be from those which are not seen.
11.4: It was by faith, Abel offered a more excellent sacrifice to God than Cain, and because of this, he received a testimonial that he was righteous, and God testified to his offering: therefore, even though he is dead, he speaks.
11.5: By faith Enoch departed and did not taste death, and he was not found, because God transferred him: but before he took him away, there was a testimonial about him, that he pleased God.
11.6: Without faith, man cannot please God: for he who comes near to God must believe that he is, and that he is a rewarder of those who seek him.
11.7: By faith Noah, when he was warned concerning the things not seen, became fearful and he made an ark to save his household, and by it he condemned the world and he became heir of righteousness which is by faith.
11.8: By faith Abraham, when he was called to depart for the land which he was to receive for an inheritance, obeyed; and he went out, not knowing where he was going.
11.9: By faith he became a sojourner in the land which was promised him as in a strange country, and he dwelt in tents with Isaac and Jacob, the heirs with him of the same promise:
11.10: For he looked for a city which has foundations, whose builder and maker is God.
11.11: Through faith also Sarah who was barren, received strength to conceive seed, and was delivered of a child when she was past age, because she was sure that he who had promised her was faithful.
11.12: Therefore, there sprang from one who was as good as dead, as many as the stars of the sky in number, and as the sand which is on the sea shore innumerable.
11.13: These all died in faith not having received the promised land, but they saw it from afar off, and rejoiced in it; and they acknowledged that they were strangers and pilgrims on earth.
11.14: For they who speak so, declare plainly that they seek a country for themselves.
11.15: And if they had a desire for that very country from which they went out, they had time to return to it again.
11.16: But now it is evident, that they desire a better city, that city which is in heaven: therefore God is not ashamed to be called their God: for he has prepared for them a city.
11.17: By faith Abraham, when he was tested, offered up Isaac: he lifted upon the altar his only begotten son, even that very one who had been received in the promise.
11.18: Of whom it was said, In Isaac shall thy seed be called:
11.19: And he reasoned in himself, It is possible for God even to raise the dead, and because of this Isaac was given to him as a parable.
11.20: By faith in the things to come Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau.
11.21: By faith Jacob, when be was dying, blessed both of the sons of Joseph, and he worshipped, leaning upon the head of his staff.
11.22: By faith Joseph, when he died made mention of the departure of the children of Israel; and gave commandment concerning his bones.
11.23: By faith the parents of Moses, hid him for three months after his birth, because they saw that the infant boy was fair; and they were not afraid of the King’s commandment.
11.24: By faith Moses, when he came to manhood, refused to be called the son of Pha’raoh’s daughter.
11.25: Choosing rather to suffer affliction with the people of God, than to enjoy the pleasures of sin for a short while.
11.26: And he reasoned that the reproach of Christ was greater riches than the treasures of Egypt: for he looked forward to be paid the reward.
11.27: By faith he forsook Egypt, not fearing the wrath of the king; and he survived after he had seen God, who is invisible.
11.28: Through faith he instituted the passover, and sprinkled the blood, lest he who destroyed the first-born should touch them.
11.29: By faith they passed through the Red Sea as by dry land: but in it the Egyptians were drowned when they made the attempt.
11.30: By faith the walls of Jericho fell down, after they were being encompassed seven days.
11.31: By faith Rahab the harlot did not perish with those who were disobedient, for she had received the spies in peace.
11.32: And what more shall I say? for time would fail me to tell of Gideon, and of Barak, and of Samson, and of Jeph’tha-e, and of David also, and Samuel, and of the rest of the prophets:
11.33: Who through faith conquered kingdoms, wrought righteousness, obtained promises, stopped the mouths of lions,
11.34: Quenched the violence of fire, escaped the edge of the sword, out of weakness were made strong, and became valiant in battle, and routed the camps of enemies;
11.35: Restored to women their sons, raised from the dead: while others died through tortures, not hoping for deliverance; that they might have a better resurrection:
11.36: Others endured mockings and scourgings, still others were delivered to bonds and imprisonment:
11.37: Others were stoned, others were sawn apart, others died by the edge of the sword: others wandered about, wearing sheep skins and goat skins; destitute, afflicted and tormented;
11.38: Of whom the world was not worthy: they wandered as though lost in the desert, and in mountains, and in dens and in caves of the earth.
11.39: Thus these all, having obtained a testimonial through the faith, did not receive the promise:
11.40: Because God, from the beginning, provided for our help lest, without us, they should not be made perfect.

12.1: THEREFORE, seeing we also are surrounded with so great a cloud of witnesses, let us lay aside every weight, and the sin which does so easily beset us, and let us run with patience the race that is set before us;
12.2: And let us look to Jesus, who was the author and the perfecter of our faith; and who, instead of the joy which he could have had, endured the cross, suffered shame, and is now seated at the right hand of the throne of God.
12.3: See, therefore, how much he has suffered from the hands of sinners, from those who were a contradiction to themselves, lest you become weary and faint in your soul.
12.4: You have not yet come face to face with blood in your striving against sin.
12.5: And you have forgotten the teaching which has been told to you as to children, My son, despise not thou the chastening of the LORD, nor let your soul faint when thou art rebuked of him,
12.6: For whom the LORD loves, he chastens him, and disciplines the sons with whom he is pleased.
12.7: Now, therefore, endure discipline, because God acts toward you as towards sons; for where is the son whom the father does not discipline?
12.8: But if you are without discipline, that very discipline by which every man is trained, then you are strangers and not sons.
12.9: Furthermore if our fathers of the flesh corrected us and we respected them, how much more then should we willingly be under subjection to our Spiritual Father, and live?
12.10: For they only for a short while, disciplined us as seemed good to them; but God corrects us for our advantage, that we might become partakers of his holiness.
12.11: No discipline, at the time, is expected to be a thing of joy, but of sorrow; but in the end it produces the fruits of peace and righteousness to those who are trained by it.
12.13: And make straight the paths for your feet, so that the weak do not go astray but are healed.
12.14: Follow peace with all men, and holiness, without which no man shall see our LORD.
12.15: Take heed lest any man among you be found short of the grace of God; or lest any root of bitterness spring forth and harm you, and thereby many be defiled;
12.16: Or lest any man among you be found immoral and weak like Esau, who sold his birthright for a morsel of meat.
12.17: For you know that afterward when he wished to inherit the blessing, he was rejected, and he had no chance of recovery, even though he sought it with tears.
12.18: For you have yet neither come near the roaring fire, nor the darkness nor the storm nor the tempest,
12.19: Nor to the sound of the trumpet and the voice of the word; which voice they heard but refused so that the word will not be spoken to them any more.
12.20: For they could not survive that which was commanded, for if even a beast drew near the mountain, it would be stoned.
12.21: And so terrible was the sight, that Moses said, I fear and quake.
12.22: But you have come near to Mount Zion, and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem, and to the innumerable multitude of angels,
12.23: And to the congregation of the first converts who are enrolled in heaven, and to God the Judge of all, and to the spirits of pious men made perfect
12.24: And to Jesus, the mediator of the new covenant, and to the sprinkling of his blood, which speaks a better message than Abel did.
12.25: Beware, therefore, lest you refuse him who speaks to you. For if they were not delivered who refused him who spoke with them on earth, much more can we not escape if we refuse him who speaks to us from heaven:
12.26: The one whose voice shook the earth; but now he has promised, saying, Once more I will shake not only the earth, but also heaven.
12.27: And this word, Once more, signifies the change of things which may be shaken, because they are made, in order that the things which can not be shaken may remain.
12.28: Therefore, we receiving a kingdom which cannot be shaken, let us hold fast that grace whereby we may serve and please God with reverence and godly fear:
12.29: For our God is a consuming fire.

13.1: LET brotherly love continue in you.
13.2: And forget not hospitality toward strangers: For thereby some were worthy to entertain angels unawares.
13.3: Remember those who are in prison, as though you were a prisoner with them; remember those who suffer adversity, for you are human also.
13.4: Marriage is honourable in all, and the bed undefiled; but God will judge those who practice vice and adultery;
13.5: Do not be carried away by the love of money; but be content with what you have: for the LORD himself has said, I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee.
13.6: So that we may boldly say, The LORD is my helper, and I will not fear what man shall do unto me.
13.7: Remember those who are your leaders, those who have spoken the word of God to you: mark the completeness of their works, and imitate their faith.
13.8: Jesus Christ the same yesterday, and today, and for ever.
13.9: Do not be carried away by strange and divers doctrines. For it is a good thing to strengthen our hearts with grace; not with food, because it did not help those who greatly sought after it.
13.10: We have an altar from which those who minister in the tabernacle have no right to eat.
13.11: For the flesh of the beasts, whose blood is brought into the sanctuary by the high priest for sin, is burned outside the camp.
13.12: Wherefore Jesus also, that he might sanctify his people with his own blood, suffered outside the city.
13.13: Let us go forth therefore to him outside the camp, bearing his reproach.
13.14: For here we have not a permanent city, but we seek one to come.
13.15: By him, therefore, let us always offer the sacrifice of praise to God, that is, the fruit of the lips giving thanks to his name.
13.16: And do not forget kindness and fellowship with the poor: for with such sacrifices God is well pleased.
13.17: Listen to your spiritual leaders, and obey them: for they are watchful guardians of your souls, as one who must give account, that they may do it with joy, and not with grief: for that is unprofitable for you.
13.18: Pray for us: for we trust we have a good conscience, in all things willing to live honestly.
13.19: But above all, I beseech you to do this that I may return to you sooner.
13.20: Now the God of peace, who brought again from the dead our LORD Jesus, that great shepherd of the sheep through the blood of the everlasting covenant,
13.21: Make you perfect in every good work to do his will, working in us that which is wellpleasing in his sight, through Jesus Christ; to whom be glory forever and ever. Amen.
13.22: And I beseech you, my brethren, to be patient in the word of comfort: for I have written you very briefly.
13.23: You should know our brother Timothy has been set at liberty; and if he should come shortly, I will see you together with him.
13.24: Salute all your spiritual leaders and all the saints. All of the brethren of Italy salute you.
13.25: Grace be with you all. Amen.
